summary:
The Almerian culture was an early Neolithic culture of southeastern Spain, particularly the province of Almería in Andalusia, dating to approximately 4500–3500 BCE. Named after the region of its densest known distribution, the Almerian culture represents one of the earliest clearly agricultural societies in the Iberian Peninsula and provides important evidence for the spread of Neolithic farming communities into southwestern Europe from their Mediterranean origins.

Almerian communities cultivated wheat and barley and herded sheep, goats, and cattle, establishing small villages in the semi-arid landscapes of southeastern Spain. Their material culture includes simple undecorated pottery, polished stone tools (ground stone axes and querns), and bone and shell ornaments. Burial practices involved collective or individual interment in stone cist graves or natural rock cavities with modest grave goods, reflecting the community's mortuary customs.

The Almerian culture was a predecessor to the more complex Millaran culture (El Argar culture's predecessor) that developed in the same region during the Chalcolithic. Southeastern Spain's proximity to North Africa and the broader western Mediterranean facilitated cultural contacts that contributed to the development of increasingly sophisticated social and technological traditions. The Almerian culture represents the early agricultural foundation upon which later Iberian Chalcolithic and Bronze Age cultures would build.
